# Part 1 — What Is This All About? {#part1}

## The Big Picture: AI, Machine Learning, and Data Mining

Before we write a single line of code, we need to understand what we are actually doing in this module.

You have probably heard words like "Artificial Intelligence", "Machine Learning", and "Data Mining" many times. They sound complicated, but they are not. Let us start from zero.

**Artificial Intelligence (AI)** is simply when a computer can do something that normally requires a human brain. That is the whole definition. Google Maps finding you the fastest route? That is AI. Your email filtering out spam? That is AI. A self-driving car? That is AI.

**Machine Learning (ML)** is one specific way of building AI systems. Instead of a programmer writing every rule by hand ("if it rains, slow down; if there is a red light, stop"), the computer learns the rules itself by looking at lots of examples. The more examples (data) you give it, the better it gets.

**Data Mining** is an older name for the same idea — extracting useful patterns from large amounts of data. In this course, treat it as the same thing as Machine Learning.

**Deep Learning** is a special, powerful type of Machine Learning that is especially good at understanding images and text. It is what powers face recognition on your phone and ChatGPT.

💡 **Think of them like Russian nesting dolls:** AI is the biggest doll. Inside it is Machine Learning. Inside Machine Learning is Deep Learning. Deep Learning ⊂ Machine Learning ⊂ Artificial Intelligence.

📌 **Remember:** A self-driving car uses AI. The part of it that "sees" the road uses Deep Learning, which is part of Machine Learning, which is part of AI.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Why Does This Matter in the Real World?

The professor has 15 years of industry experience. Here is where machine learning is used every single day:

**Marketing:** A company has 1 million customers. Instead of sending the same email to everyone, a machine learning model predicts which customers are likely to buy, and sends targeted offers only to those people. This saves money and gets better results.

**Fraud Detection:** Your credit card company processes millions of transactions per day. 99.999% are normal. A tiny fraction are fraudulent. A machine learning model watches every transaction in real time and raises an alarm when something looks suspicious — all automatically, in milliseconds.

**Healthcare:** A doctor cannot review every patient's scan manually fast enough. A machine learning model can scan thousands of medical images and flag the ones that look unusual for a doctor to review. It does not replace the doctor — it helps the doctor focus on what matters.

**Customer Churn:** A phone company wants to know which customers are about to cancel their contract. A model predicts this in advance so the company can call those customers and offer them a better deal before they leave.

**Manufacturing:** Sensors on a factory machine monitor vibrations, temperature, and pressure. A model learns what "normal" looks like. When the readings become unusual, it predicts a breakdown before it happens. The factory saves millions by fixing the machine before it fails rather than after.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## How a Machine Learning Project Actually Works — CRISP-DM

When you start a machine learning project, you need a plan. Machine learning is not like regular software development. You cannot say "we will finish by Friday." It is research — you explore, discover, go back, and try again.

The standard process used in industry is called **CRISP-DM** — Cross Industry Standard Process for Data Mining. It has six steps, and crucially, it is a **circle** — you can always go back to a previous step.

```         
1. Business Understanding  →  What problem are we solving?
2. Data Understanding      →  What data do we have? Is it any good?
3. Data Preparation        →  Clean and prepare the data.
4. Modelling               →  Train a machine learning algorithm.
5. Evaluation              →  How well does it work?
6. Deployment              →  Put it into use in the real world.
         ↑_______________________________↓  (repeat if needed)
```

💡 **Building a House Analogy:** Step 1 (Business Understanding) = What does the client want? How many bedrooms? Step 2 (Data Understanding) = What materials do we have? Are they good quality? Step 3 (Data Preparation) = Cut the wood, mix the concrete, prepare everything. Step 4 (Modelling) = Actually build the house. Step 5 (Evaluation) = Walk through and check everything is solid. Step 6 (Deployment) = The client moves in. But if you discover a problem in Step 4 — for example, the walls are crooked — you go back to Step 3 and prepare differently.

⚠️ **Important detail:** There are **two separate data steps** (Step 2 AND Step 3) before you even start modelling. Many beginners jump straight to modelling with dirty data. This almost always produces bad results.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Three Types of Machine Learning

All machine learning tasks fall into one of three categories. Understanding which type you are doing is the first decision you make in any project.

### Type 1 — Supervised Learning

You show the computer lots of examples, and each example has a **correct answer label** already attached.

💡 **Teaching a Child:** You show a child 100 photos of cats (labelled "cat") and 100 photos of dogs (labelled "dog"). After seeing enough examples, the child can tell the difference. You supervised the learning by providing the labels.

In supervised learning: - The **input columns** (X) are called predictor variables or features - The **column you want to predict** (Y) is called the target variable - The process of building the model is called **training** or **model building** - Once trained, you use the model on new data to make predictions

There are two kinds of supervised learning:

**Classification** = predicting a category. The answer is one of a fixed set of options. Examples: Will this email be spam (yes/no)? Will this customer leave (yes/no)? What flower species is this (Setosa/Versicolor/Virginica)?

**Regression** = predicting a number. The answer can be any value on a scale. Examples: What will this house sell for (€320,000)? What will the temperature be tomorrow (14°C)? How many units will we sell this month (4,200)?

### Type 2 — Unsupervised Learning

There are no labels. The computer must find patterns on its own.

💡 **The New Teacher:** A new teacher gets a class of 30 students and has never met them before. Without any grades or labels, they can still notice: "These 5 students always sit together and seem similar." They have found a cluster — without anyone telling them to.

The main unsupervised task in this course is **clustering** (also called segmentation) — finding natural groups in data.

### Type 3 — Reinforcement Learning

The computer learns by trial and error. It tries an action, gets a reward if it was good, and a penalty if it was bad. Over time, it learns to make better decisions.

💡 **Learning to Ride a Bike:** Nobody tells you the rules of balance. You fall, you learn. You stay upright, you are rewarded. Eventually you master it. This is reinforcement learning.

Examples: AlphaGo (the AI that beat the world chess and Go champions), game-playing AI, self-driving car steering.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 2 — Understanding Your Data Before Anything Else {#part2}

## Why You Must Look at Your Data First

This is possibly the most important lesson in the whole module: **always explore your data before building any model.**

The professor said it clearly: "If you are just producing plots and then jumping straight into machine learning, you are wasting your time. You should be looking at the plots, interpreting them, and letting them guide you."

Exploring your data is called **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)**. Think of it as getting to know your data before you trust it.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Mean vs Median — Which One Tells the Truth?

These are the two most basic ways to describe "the typical value" in a column.

**Mean** = add everything up, divide by how many there are. This is the average.

**Median** = sort all values from smallest to largest, take the one in the middle.

They give different answers. Which one is more honest depends on your data.

💡 **The Billionaire Problem:** Imagine 10 people in a room. Nine of them earn between €25,000 and €60,000. The average salary in the room is about €38,000. That makes sense — it represents everyone fairly.

Now a billionaire walks in. His annual income is €500,000,000. The new average = roughly €50,000,000. That is completely misleading! Nobody in the room earns anywhere near that. The one extreme value (the billionaire) has destroyed the average.

But the **median** barely changes. It is still the salary of the person who is exactly in the middle of the sorted list — about €40,000. It represents the "typical" person in the room much better.

📌 **Rule:** When data has outliers or is lopsided (skewed), use the **median**. When data is balanced and symmetric, the **mean** is fine.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Quartiles and the IQR — Understanding Spread

The **median** tells us where the middle is. But where is everything else?

**Quartiles** divide your sorted data into four equal parts: - **Q1** = the value where 25% of the data is below it - **Q2** = the median (50% below, 50% above) - **Q3** = the value where 75% of the data is below it

The **Interquartile Range (IQR)** = Q3 − Q1. It tells you how spread out the middle 50% of your data is.

💡 **Class Test Scores:** Imagine 100 students take an exam. Sort their scores from lowest to highest. Q1 = the score of the 25th student = 45 marks Q3 = the score of the 75th student = 72 marks IQR = 72 − 45 = 27 marks This means the middle half of the class scored within a 27-mark range. It tells you how bunched together or spread out the typical students are.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Three Charts You Will Use Every Day

These three charts all show the **distribution** of one column (how the values spread out). They show the same information in different ways.

### Histogram

A bar chart where each bar shows how many data points fall in a particular range. Tall bar = many values in that range. Short bar = few values.

Use it to see the overall **shape** of your data.

### Density Plot

The same idea as a histogram, but drawn as a smooth curve instead of bars. Easier to see the shape clearly.

### Boxplot

The most information-dense of the three, but the hardest to read at first.

```         
        |←————IQR————→|
  ——————|=======|======|——————    ●  ← outlier
        Q1    Median   Q3
        ↑                ↑
     Q1-1.5×IQR      Q3+1.5×IQR
     (lower whisker) (upper whisker)
```

-   The **box** = the middle 50% of the data (Q1 to Q3)
-   The **line inside the box** = the median
-   The **whiskers** = the range, excluding outliers
-   The **dots outside** = outliers (unusually high or low values)

⚠️ **Practical advice from the professor:** Boxplots are very useful for you as a data scientist. But **never show them to non-technical business people** — they do not understand them. Use simple bar charts or line charts for presentations.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Normal Distribution vs Skewed Data

The **normal distribution** (also called the bell curve) is the most important shape in all of statistics.

Most real-world measurements follow it naturally: heights of people, errors in measurements, exam scores. It looks like a symmetrical bell — most values cluster around the middle, and fewer values are at the extremes.

Why does this matter? Many machine learning algorithms work best — or even assume — that your data follows a normal distribution. If your data is very lopsided, those algorithms will not work well.

**Skewed data** is data where the distribution is pulled to one side:

-   **Right-skewed** = most values are small, but a few are very large. The mean is pulled upward and is larger than the median. Examples: income, house prices, transaction values.
-   **Left-skewed** = most values are large, but a few are very small. The mean is pulled downward.

💡 **How to spot skew in a chart:** Look at which side has the longer "tail." If the tail goes to the right — right-skewed. In a boxplot, if the median line is not in the centre of the box, the data is skewed.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Fixing Skewed Data — The Log Transformation

When data is right-skewed, a simple and powerful fix is the **log transformation**.

``` r
mammals$log_body_wt <- log(mammals$body_wt)
```

💡 **Mammals Example from the course:** The mammals dataset has body weight and brain weight for 62 species. Most mammals are small (mice, rabbits), but elephants weigh 6,000+ kg. This makes the data extremely skewed.

Without log transform: R² = 0.87 After log transform: R² = 0.92

A 35% improvement in model accuracy. From one single line of code. That is the power of understanding your data.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Bimodal Distribution — The Red Flag

A **bimodal distribution** has **two peaks** in the histogram. This is a red flag.

It almost always means two different groups have been mixed together in one column.

💡 **Height Example:** If you measure the height of all students in a school and plot a histogram, you might see two humps — one around 160cm (girls) and one around 175cm (boys). The data is bimodal because two distinct populations are mixed.

💡 **Iris Dataset Example:** When you plot `Petal.Length` for all iris flowers, you see two peaks. Why? The Setosa species has tiny petals (peak on the left). Versicolor and Virginica have much longer petals (peak on the right). The model will likely find it very easy to separate Setosa from the others.

📌 **Whenever you see two peaks in a histogram, ask: "Are there two types of things mixed together in my data?"**

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Looking at Two Columns at Once — Scatter Plots and Correlation

**Univariate analysis** looks at one column at a time (histograms, density plots, boxplots). **Multivariate analysis** looks at two or more columns together.

A **scatter plot** puts two columns on the x and y axes and draws a dot for each row. If the dots form a diagonal line, those two things are related. If the dots are scattered randomly, there is no relationship.

**Correlation** measures the strength of the linear relationship between two columns: - From −1 to +1 - **+1** = perfect positive relationship (both go up together) - **−1** = perfect negative relationship (one goes up, the other goes down) - **0** = no relationship

The `ggpairs()` function from the GGally library shows scatter plots, histograms, and correlations for multiple columns all at once — use it as your first step in any new dataset.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 3 — Preparing Your Data — Feature Engineering {#part3}

## What is Feature Engineering?

**Feature engineering** is the process of transforming your raw data into better inputs for your machine learning model. It happens after data exploration and before modelling.

The professor said something very important: **"Feature engineering is more important than your choice of algorithm."**

💡 **The Car Race Analogy:** Even the most powerful Formula 1 car will not win a race if you put dirty, low-quality fuel in it. Feature engineering is the fuel. A well-prepared dataset with a simple algorithm will almost always beat a badly-prepared dataset with a complicated algorithm.

Feature engineering includes: - **Removing useless columns** — passenger ID, name, ticket number. These have no predictive power. - **Converting column types** — e.g., ticket class should be a category (1st, 2nd, 3rd), not a number - **Filling in missing values** (imputation) - **Transforming skewed columns** (log transformation) - **Creating new columns** from existing ones - **Encoding text columns** as numbers (one-hot encoding) - **Scaling** numeric columns to a similar range

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Handling Missing Values — Imputation

Real-world data almost always has missing values. The Age column in the Titanic dataset, for example, is missing for many passengers.

Most machine learning algorithms simply cannot handle missing values — they will crash or give wrong results.

**Imputation** means filling in a reasonable estimate for the missing values.

**Simple method — Use the mean:**

``` r
meanAge <- mean(train_data$Age, na.rm = TRUE)
train_data$Age[is.na(train_data$Age)] <- meanAge
```

**Better method — Use a group mean:** Instead of giving everyone the same average age of 29, calculate the average age by **gender AND title** (Mr, Mrs, Miss, Master). A "Master" (boy under 12) should get a much lower age than a "Mr."

⚠️ **Critical rule: Split your data FIRST, then impute.**

``` r
# WRONG — this is data leakage!
df$Age[is.na(df$Age)] <- mean(df$Age, na.rm = TRUE)  # uses all data!
train <- df[train_idx, ]
test  <- df[test_idx,  ]

# CORRECT — split first, impute from training only
train <- df[train_idx, ]
test  <- df[test_idx,  ]
meanAge <- mean(train$Age, na.rm = TRUE)  # from training ONLY
train$Age[is.na(train$Age)] <- meanAge
test$Age[is.na(test$Age)]   <- meanAge   # apply SAME value to test
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## One-Hot Encoding — Turning Words into Numbers

Most machine learning algorithms only understand numbers. But many columns contain text like "male"/"female" or "Embarked: S/C/Q."

**One-Hot Encoding** converts each text value into its own numeric column filled with 0s and 1s.

```         
Original:        After encoding:
Gender           Gender_female    Gender_male
------           -------------    -----------
male       →           0               1
female     →           1               0
```

Each row gets a 1 in the column for its value, and 0 in all others.

``` r
library(caret)
dmy <- dummyVars(" ~ .", data = train_data)
train_encoded <- data.frame(predict(dmy, newdata = train_data))
```

📌 **Why not just use 1 for male and 2 for female?** Because that would imply female is "twice as much" as male — which is nonsense. One-hot encoding avoids this problem.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Data Leakage — The Silent Killer of Models

**Data leakage** is when information that would not be available in the real world gets into your training data. The model appears to work brilliantly — but only because it is cheating.

💡 **The Carpathia Example:** The class ran a decision tree on the Titanic dataset and accidentally included a column called "Carpathia" — the name of the rescue ship that picked up survivors. The model got nearly 100% accuracy! But the problem is: in the real world, you would not know which rescue ship saved someone before the Titanic sank. The column is a result of survival, not a predictor of it. This is leakage.

**Three types of leakage:**

1.  **Direct leakage** — a column is essentially the same as the target variable
2.  **Process leakage** — you do data preparation (like imputation) using the entire dataset before splitting into train/test
3.  **Temporal leakage** — you use data from the future to predict the past (e.g., a "card cancelled" flag that was set after the fraud occurred)

📌 **Golden Rule: Always split your data into train and test FIRST. Then do all data preparation using only the training set.**

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 4 — Building Your First Models {#part4}

## The Train/Test Split — The Most Important Rule in ML

Before you build any model, you must split your data into two completely separate groups:

**Training set (90%)** — the model learns from this. It sees all the data. **Test set (10%)** — you evaluate the model on this. The model has NEVER seen it.

💡 **The Exam Analogy:** A student who memorises the exact answers to last year's exam questions can score 100% — but if this year's exam has slightly different questions, they will fail. A model evaluated on its own training data will always look perfect — but will fail on real new data.

``` r
set.seed(42)            # ensures the same random split every time
nobs  <- nrow(iris)
train <- sample(nobs, 0.9 * nobs)
test  <- setdiff(seq_len(nobs), train)

train_data <- iris[train, ]
test_data  <- iris[test,  ]
```

⚠️ **Never, ever evaluate your model on data it was trained on. You will always get an unrealistically high score.**

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Decision Trees — The Most Interpretable Model

A **decision tree** is a model that makes predictions by asking a series of yes/no questions.

💡 **The 20 Questions Game:** You think of an animal. I ask: "Does it have four legs?" → Yes. "Is it bigger than a car?" → No. "Does it have a tail?" → Yes. "Is it a cat or a dog?" → Cat! I guessed it by asking yes/no questions. A decision tree does exactly this with your data.

Each decision point in the tree is called a **node**. You go left if the condition is true, right if it is false. The final answer at the bottom of the tree is called a **leaf node**.

**Why decision trees are loved:** They are **interpretable** — you can look at the tree and understand exactly why the model made each prediction. You can even write the tree's rules as simple `if-then` code in any programming language.

💡 **Titanic Decision Tree:** The tree might learn: "If you are male → look at your age. If age \> 12, predict NOT survived." Then: "If you are female → look at ticket class. If class 1 or 2, predict SURVIVED." This matches historical reality perfectly — women and first-class passengers had priority on lifeboats.

**Why decision trees have limits:** They are **greedy** — at each step they pick the best column available right now, without thinking about the future. Once a column is used, it is "off the table." This makes them bad at finding interactions between variables (e.g., "being female AND first class gives an extra boost").

They also tend to **overfit** — they can grow so large and specific that they memorise the training data instead of learning general patterns. The fix is **pruning** — cutting off the bottom branches to simplify the tree.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Confusion Matrix — Understanding Your Results

After your model makes predictions on the test set, you need to compare the predictions to the actual answers. This comparison is shown in a **Confusion Matrix**.

```         
                   ACTUAL: 0          ACTUAL: 1
PREDICTED: 0    True Negative (TN)   False Negative (FN)
PREDICTED: 1    False Positive (FP)  True Positive (TP)
```

-   **True Positive (TP):** Model said YES. Answer is YES. ✅ Correct.
-   **True Negative (TN):** Model said NO. Answer is NO. ✅ Correct.
-   **False Positive (FP):** Model said YES. Answer is NO. ❌ Wrong. (Also called a "false alarm")
-   **False Negative (FN):** Model said NO. Answer is YES. ❌ Wrong. (The model missed it)

💡 **Memory trick:** Anything starting with **True** = the model was **right**. Anything starting with **False** = the model was **wrong**. The second word (Positive/Negative) = what the model **predicted**.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Accuracy — Simple but Often Wrong

```         
Accuracy = (TP + TN) / (TP + TN + FP + FN)
         = correct predictions / all predictions
```

It is simple to understand and explain. But it is only valid when errors of all types have the same cost.

💡 **The Fraud Detection Trap:** Imagine 1,000,000 credit card transactions. Only 10 are fraudulent. A model that says "NOT fraud" for EVERY SINGLE transaction has 99.999% accuracy. But it found zero fraudulent transactions. It is completely useless. Accuracy is a terrible metric here because the classes are so unequal.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Precision, Recall, and F1 — Better Metrics

When classes are unequal, or when one type of error is much worse than the other, use these three metrics instead.

**Precision** — "Of all the cases I predicted as positive, how many actually were positive?"

```         
Precision = TP / (TP + FP)
```

💡 **Medical Test Analogy for Precision:** The model screened 100 people and said 20 of them are sick. Of those 20, only 16 were actually sick (4 were healthy but flagged by mistake). Precision = 16/20 = 80%. When the model says "sick," it is right 80% of the time.

**Recall** — "Of all the actual positive cases, how many did I find?"

```         
Recall = TP / (TP + FN)
```

💡 **Medical Test Analogy for Recall:** There were actually 20 sick people in total. The model found 16 of them. 4 were missed (false negatives). Recall = 16/20 = 80%. The model caught 80% of all the actually sick people.

**The Precision-Recall Trade-off:** You cannot maximise both at the same time. If you lower the threshold (predict positive more aggressively), recall goes up but precision goes down. If you raise the threshold, precision goes up but recall goes down. The right balance depends on your problem.

💡 **Which matters more in medicine?** Recall matters more. It is better to accidentally flag a healthy person (low precision) than to miss a sick person (low recall). A missed cancer diagnosis could be fatal.

**F1 Score** — the balanced combination:

```         
F1 = 2 × (Precision × Recall) / (Precision + Recall)
```

The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall. It punishes extreme cases — a model with precision = 1.0 and recall = 0.0 would have a simple average of 0.5, but an F1 score of 0.0. Correctly showing it is a terrible model.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Logistic Regression — Classification with Probabilities

Despite the name, logistic regression is a **classification** algorithm, not a regression one.

Instead of predicting a category directly, it predicts the **probability** that something belongs to a class. You then apply a threshold (usually 50%) to convert that probability into a yes/no answer.

💡 **The Doctor's Probability:** A doctor does not just say "you are sick" or "you are healthy." They might say "you have a 73% chance of having this condition based on your symptoms." Logistic regression works the same way — it gives you a probability, not just a label.

The probability comes from a mathematical curve called the **sigmoid function**, which squeezes any number into the range 0 to 1.

``` r
glm_model <- glm(Survived ~ ., data = train_data,
                 family = binomial(link = "logit"))
pr <- predict(glm_model, newdata = test_data, type = "response")
pr <- ifelse(pr >= 0.5, 1, 0)  # convert probability to label
```

When you look at `summary(glm_model)`, look for the **stars (**\*)\*\*. Three stars means that variable is very important. A dot or blank means it barely contributes — consider removing it.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## ROC Curve and AUC — Seeing the Full Picture

The **ROC curve** (Receiver Operating Characteristic) shows how your model performs at every possible threshold, not just 0.5.

-   The x-axis = False Positive Rate (how often you raise a false alarm)
-   The y-axis = True Positive Rate / Recall (how many real positives you catch)
-   A perfect model curves into the top-left corner
-   A useless model goes diagonally (no better than a coin flip)

**AUC** (Area Under the Curve) = a single number summarising the curve. - AUC = 1.0 → perfect - AUC = 0.5 → useless (random guessing) - AUC = 0.81 (as in the Titanic logistic regression example) → good

📌 **AUC is your go-to metric when data is imbalanced** — it is not affected by unequal class sizes the way accuracy is.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Overfitting vs Underfitting — The Most Common Mistake

These two problems affect every model you will ever build. Understanding them is essential.

**Underfitting (High Bias)** — the model is too simple. It has not learned the patterns in the data. It performs badly on BOTH the training set and the test set.

**Overfitting (High Variance)** — the model is too complex. It has memorised the training data so well that it cannot generalise to new data. It performs brilliantly on the training set but poorly on the test set.

💡 **The Dartboard:** Underfitting = your darts are all over the board. No consistency whatsoever. Overfitting = your darts all land in the same spot, but in the wrong place. Good model = your darts cluster tightly around the bullseye.

💡 **The Student:** Underfitting = student never studied. Fails both the practice exam and the real exam. Overfitting = student memorised the exact answers to last year's exam. Gets 100% on the practice exam, but fails the real exam because the questions are slightly different. Good model = student understood the concepts. Does well on both.

**How to diagnose:** - Training accuracy low AND test accuracy low → underfitting - Training accuracy high BUT test accuracy much lower → overfitting

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 5 — More Algorithms {#part5}

## Naive Bayes

Naive Bayes uses **probability** to classify things. It calculates: "Given these inputs, how probable is each class?"

💡 **Spam Email:** Over time, you notice that spam emails often contain the words "free," "win," and "prize." Naive Bayes calculates: if this email contains all three words, the probability it is spam is 95%. It is called "naive" because it assumes each word is independent of the others — which is not always true, but it works surprisingly well in practice.

Best for: text classification, spam detection, small datasets. Limitation: the independence assumption is often unrealistic.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Support Vector Machines (SVM)

An SVM finds the **widest possible boundary** between two classes.

💡 **The Road:** Imagine two groups of people standing on a large field — red group and blue group. Draw a road between them. An SVM finds the widest possible road (the maximum margin) that separates the two groups, making it clearest which side each person belongs to. The people standing right next to the road on each side are called **support vectors** — they define where the road goes.

The **kernel trick** allows SVM to handle data that is not linearly separable. It mathematically transforms the data into a higher dimension where a straight boundary can separate the classes.

Best for: high accuracy on medium-sized datasets, handles outliers well. Limitation: slow on very large datasets (millions of rows).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Random Forest

A **Random Forest** builds hundreds of decision trees and combines their votes.

💡 **The Wisdom of the Crowd:** You ask 500 different people the same question. Each person has different knowledge and experiences. The majority answer is usually more reliable than any single person's answer. Random Forest works the same way — 500 trees, each seeing slightly different data, each giving a vote. The majority vote wins.

Each tree is built on a **random sample** of rows AND a **random subset of columns**. This ensures that each tree is slightly different and finds different patterns. The combination is much more powerful than any single tree.

It also shows you **feature importance** — which columns matter most for the prediction. This is invaluable for understanding your data.

Best for: powerful, handles interactions between variables, robust, shows feature importance. Limitation: less interpretable than a single decision tree.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Gradient Boosted Machines (GBM)

GBM also combines many trees, but in a completely different way from Random Forest.

💡 **The Improving Student:** You take an exam, get your results, and the teacher highlights every question you got wrong. On the next exam, you focus specifically on those weak areas. You take that exam, get more results, and focus on the NEW mistakes. With each round, you get better specifically at your weak spots. GBM works exactly this way — each new tree focuses on the errors of the previous tree, correcting them one by one.

📌 **The professor says this clearly:** From 15 years of industry experience, the top three algorithms for tabular data (rows and columns) are: **SVM, Random Forest, and GBM**. If you are building a model for a real business problem with structured data, start with these three.

Best for: often the best accuracy on tabular data, wins many Kaggle competitions. Limitation: slower and harder to tune than Random Forest, less intuitive.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Neural Networks

A neural network is loosely inspired by the human brain. Information passes through layers of interconnected "neurons," each doing a small calculation, until the final layer outputs a prediction.

💡 **The Assembly Line:** Each worker (neuron) on the assembly line does one specific small task and passes the result to the next worker. The final worker at the end of the line produces the finished product (the prediction). There can be hundreds of layers and millions of workers.

Best for: images, text, audio, very large datasets. Limitation: needs a LOT of data, requires all-numeric inputs carefully scaled, extremely hard to interpret ("black box"), expensive to train.

⚠️ **For most business problems with tabular data, Random Forest or GBM will outperform a neural network** — with much less effort.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## LDA and QDA — When Your Data Is Well-Behaved

**LDA (Linear Discriminant Analysis)** draws a straight line between classes. **QDA (Quadratic Discriminant Analysis)** draws a curved line.

These work best when your data is approximately normally distributed. LDA assumes all classes have the same shape. QDA allows each class its own shape but needs more data.

💡 **Separating Two Clusters of Sand:** LDA uses a straight ruler to divide two piles of sand. QDA uses a flexible curved wire. If the piles are oval and different sizes, the curved wire fits better.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 6 — Fixing Problems in Your Data and Models {#part6}

## Imbalanced Datasets

An **imbalanced dataset** has far more examples of one class than the other.

Examples: 99.9% normal transactions vs 0.1% fraud. 95% healthy patients vs 5% sick patients.

The problem: most algorithms will simply learn to predict the majority class all the time and ignore the minority class. They will get high accuracy but terrible recall.

**Solutions:**

**1. Adjust the threshold.** Instead of predicting positive when probability ≥ 0.5, lower the threshold to 0.3 or 0.2. This captures more of the minority class (higher recall) at the cost of more false alarms (lower precision).

**2. Upsample the minority class.** Duplicate rows from the minority class until both classes are balanced. Use `replace = TRUE` so you can duplicate freely.

**3. Downsample the majority class.** Randomly remove rows from the majority class.

**4. SMOTE.** Creates synthetic new examples of the minority class (smarter than just duplicating).

⚠️ **Always balance only the training data. Never touch the test set.** The test set must reflect the real-world distribution to give honest evaluation scores.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Sampling and Cross-Validation

**Sampling with replacement** means a row can be selected multiple times. This is used in Random Forest and GBM. From 100 rows, you select 100 with replacement — about 63% of rows are unique, the rest are duplicates.

**Sampling without replacement** means each row is selected at most once. This is used for the train/test split.

**K-Fold Cross-Validation** is a more reliable way to evaluate your model than a single train/test split:

1.  Divide the data into K equal groups (folds), typically K = 5 or 10
2.  Train on K-1 folds, test on the remaining one
3.  Repeat K times — each fold takes a turn as the test set
4.  Average the K accuracy scores

💡 **Why is this better?** With a single split, you might get unlucky — your test set might be unusually easy or unusually hard. Cross-validation tests on every part of the data and averages the results, giving a much more reliable estimate of real performance.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Hyperparameters — The Last Step, Not the First

**Parameters** are values the model learns by itself during training (e.g., the weights in a neural network, the coefficients in regression).

**Hyperparameters** are settings you choose before training that control how the model learns (e.g., how many trees to build in a Random Forest, how deep to allow trees to grow).

Hyperparameter tuning = trying many combinations and picking the best.

⚠️ **The biggest mistake beginners make:** They jump straight to hyperparameter tuning thinking it will dramatically improve their results. It will not — not if the data preparation is poor. The professor said it clearly: fix your data first. Good feature engineering gives you 80–90% of your improvement. Hyperparameter tuning gives you the last 5–10%.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 7 — Finding Groups: Clustering and Segmentation {#part7}

## What is Clustering?

Clustering is an **unsupervised** task — there is no target variable. The algorithm finds natural groups in the data based on similarity.

💡 **The New Gym:** A gym just opened. They have 5,000 members but know nothing about them. They run clustering. They discover 4 groups: morning yoga people, lunchtime runners, evening weightlifters, and weekend beginners. Now they can schedule classes, target marketing, and design programmes for each group — without anyone having told the algorithm what these groups were.

⚠️ **Never expect equal cluster sizes!** Clustering finds natural groups — it does not try to make them equal. You might get one huge cluster with 70% of the data and small clusters with 5–10%. The small clusters are often the most interesting and unusual customers.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## K-Means Clustering — Step by Step

K-means is the most popular clustering algorithm. You must choose K (the number of clusters) before starting.

1.  Place K random points in your data space as starting centres (centroids)
2.  Assign each data point to its nearest centroid
3.  Move each centroid to the average position of all its assigned points
4.  Repeat steps 2-3 until nothing moves

The goal is to minimise **within-cluster variance** — make points inside each cluster as close to each other as possible.

💡 **The Flag Analogy:** Imagine placing 3 flags randomly on a map representing your customers. Every customer "joins" their nearest flag. Then each flag moves to the centre of its group. Then customers rejoin their new nearest flag. Flags move again. Repeat until flags stop moving. Those are your cluster centres.

⚠️ **Always scale your data before clustering!** If one column is in euros (0–100,000) and another is age (0–100), the euros column will completely dominate the clustering. Scaling makes all columns equally important.

``` r
iris_scaled <- scale(iris[, 1:4])  # scale to mean=0, sd=1
km <- kmeans(iris_scaled, centers = 3, nstart = 25)
```

To choose K, use the **Elbow Method**: plot within-cluster variance for different values of K. Choose the K where the curve bends — adding more clusters after that point does not help much.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Hierarchical Clustering — No Need to Choose K

Hierarchical clustering works differently. It starts with every single point in its own cluster, then merges the two closest clusters, repeatedly, until everything is one big cluster.

The result is shown as a **dendrogram** (a branching tree diagram). You then cut the tree at whatever height gives you the number of clusters you want.

**Advantage:** You do not need to decide K upfront — you look at the dendrogram and choose. **Disadvantage:** Slow on very large datasets.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## After Clustering — What Do the Groups Mean?

The algorithm gives you cluster numbers (1, 2, 3...) but those numbers mean nothing by themselves. Your job is to **interpret** what each cluster represents.

Use your Week 2 tools — boxplots, scatter plots, histograms — but now colour/group by cluster instead of by species.

In retail loyalty, companies give their clusters nicknames: "Young Spenders," "Loyal Elders," "Weekend Browsers." Then marketing designs targeted campaigns for each group.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 8 — Recommendation Engines {#part8}

## How Does Amazon Know What You Want to Buy?

You have seen it a thousand times. You look at a book on Amazon and it says: "People who bought this also bought..." Or Netflix says "Because you watched X, you might like Y." How does that work?

### Market Basket Analysis (Apriori Algorithm)

This approach looks at what items are **bought together** in the same transaction (basket).

💡 **Supermarket Layout:** A supermarket analysed its receipts and noticed that people who buy nappies often also buy beer. Why? Parents of newborns send one parent to get nappies in the evening. While there, they also pick up a beer. The supermarket moved beer closer to the nappies section. Sales of beer increased. This is market basket analysis in action.

Three key numbers: - **Support** = what percentage of all transactions contain this combination? - **Confidence** = given that someone bought A, how likely are they to also buy B? - **Lift** = how much more likely is B bought with A, compared to B being bought by anyone? (Lift \> 1 = a real positive association)

### Collaborative Filtering

Instead of looking at items bought together, collaborative filtering looks at **similar users**.

💡 **The Friend Recommendation:** You and your friend have watched almost all the same movies and rated them similarly. Your friend has watched one movie you have not seen and loved it. Collaborative filtering predicts: "Since your friend loved this and you have similar taste, you will probably love it too."

The challenge is the **sparse matrix problem**: if a streaming service has 100,000 movies and an average user watches 50, then 99.95% of the possible "ratings" are empty. Finding patterns in such a sparse matrix is technically difficult — it was so hard that Netflix offered a **\$1,000,000 prize** in 2006 to anyone who could improve their algorithm by 10%.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 9 — Dimensionality Reduction {#part9}

## The Problem of Too Many Columns

**Dimensionality** = the number of columns (features) in your dataset.

A dataset with 10 columns is easy to work with. A dataset with 784 columns (like an image of 28×28 pixels) or 100,000 columns (like a movie rating database) is a serious problem for most algorithms.

As the number of columns grows, algorithms get slower — not linearly, but exponentially. Doubling the columns might make the algorithm 8 times slower. The professor knew this problem well from his Master's thesis on high-dimensional data.

Additionally, many algorithms assume there are enough data points to fill the space. When you have thousands of columns, the data becomes very sparse — points are far apart from each other in every direction, making it hard to find patterns.

**Dimensionality Reduction** solves this by transforming many columns into fewer columns while keeping as much useful information as possible.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## PCA — Principal Component Analysis

**PCA** is the most popular dimensionality reduction technique.

It takes your many (possibly correlated) columns and creates a smaller set of **new columns** called **Principal Components (PCs)**. Each PC is a mathematical combination of the original columns.

-   PC1 captures the **most variance** (the most information) in the data
-   PC2 captures the second most, and so on
-   Each PC is completely uncorrelated with the others

💡 **The Shadow Analogy:** Imagine a 3D object (a sculpture) lit by sunlight. Its shadow on the floor is a 2D shape. The shadow captures most of the important information about the sculpture's outline, even though it has lost one dimension. PCA finds the best "shadow" of your high-dimensional data.

💡 **Iris Example:** The Iris dataset has 4 columns. PCA finds that 2 principal components can explain most of the variation. You can now plot 150 flowers in a 2D chart instead of a 4D space — and the three species are clearly separated.

**The Scree Plot** shows how much variance each PC explains. You look for the "elbow" — where the curve flattens — and keep only the PCs before that point. Typically you aim to keep enough PCs to explain 80–95% of the total variance.

⚠️ **Always fit PCA on training data only.** Apply the same transformation to the test set. This is the same rule as imputation — never let test data influence your preparation steps.

⚠️ **Limitation:** PCA components are hard to interpret. You cannot say "PC1 = age" or "PC2 = income." They are mathematical combinations with no simple meaning.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## t-SNE — Visualising High-Dimensional Data

**t-SNE** compresses data into exactly 2 dimensions so you can make a scatter plot and see if natural groups exist.

Use it for exploration only — it is not suitable as input for machine learning models. Think of it as a sophisticated way to "look" at your data.

💡 **The Map:** t-SNE is like making a 2D map of a complicated 3D landscape. The map is a simplified but useful representation. You would not give the map to a GPS algorithm — but you would use it to understand the overall lay of the land.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 10 — Finding the Unusual: Anomaly Detection {#part10}

## What is Anomaly Detection?

**Anomaly detection** (also called outlier analysis) finds data points that are very different from all the others. No labels are needed — it is unsupervised.

💡 **The Security Camera:** A security camera in a shop records footage for weeks. It learns what "normal" activity looks like — people walking in, browsing, paying at the till. One day, someone behaves completely differently (going to places other customers never go, acting erratically). The camera flags this as an anomaly. It did not need to be told what suspicious behaviour looks like — it just knows what normal looks like and flags what does not fit.

**Where it is used:** - Fraud detection — a transaction is very different from this customer's normal behaviour - Machine health — a sensor reading is outside the normal range - Data quality — a data entry error (someone typed 999 instead of 99 for age) - Image quality — a blurry or mislabelled photo in a training dataset

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## PCA for Anomaly Detection

The clever approach taught in this course combines PCA with anomaly detection:

1.  Run PCA on the data to compress it
2.  "Reconstruct" the original data from the compressed version
3.  Calculate the **reconstruction error** (how different is the reconstructed data from the original?)
4.  Normal records reconstruct well → low error. Unusual records do not → high error.

💡 **The MNIST Handwriting Example:** The course used a dataset of handwritten digit "5s." After running PCA + anomaly detection: - Images with **low reconstruction error** = typical, clear 5s — easy to read - Images with **high reconstruction error** = very thin strokes, very thick strokes, or ambiguous shapes — unusual 5s that even humans might misread

This technique can automatically flag problematic training examples in an image dataset.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 11 — Graph Analytics {#part11}

## A Different Way to Think About Data

Traditional databases store data in tables. Rows and columns. Every row has the same columns.

**Graph analytics** is fundamentally different. It focuses on the **relationships** between things, not the things themselves.

**Nodes (vertices)** = the things (people, products, accounts, web pages) **Edges (links)** = the relationships between them (bought, follows, linked to, transferred money to)

💡 **Social Network:** Every person is a node. Every friendship is an edge. Now you can ask questions like: "Who is connected to the most people?" (influencer analysis). "Who is the most important bridge between two groups?" (vulnerability analysis). "Is this group of 5 accounts all connected to the same suspicious node?" (fraud ring detection).

**Where graph analytics is used:** - Marketing attribution (tracing which ad led to a purchase through a chain of events) - Fraud rings (multiple accounts connected in unusual patterns) - Route planning (Google Maps — every intersection is a node, every road is an edge) - Recommendation engines (which users are connected through similar purchases?)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 12 — Putting It All Together {#part12}

## The Full Machine Learning Pipeline

Here is the complete journey from raw data to a working model:

```         
Raw Data
    ↓
1. EXPLORE (Week 2)
   - Histograms, density plots, boxplots
   - Means, medians, correlations
   - Find skew, outliers, missing values, bimodal distributions
    ↓
2. PREPARE / FEATURE ENGINEER (Week 5)
   - Remove useless columns
   - Fix column types
   - Impute missing values (from training data only!)
   - Log-transform skewed columns
   - One-hot encode text columns
   - Scale numeric columns
    ↓
3. SPLIT INTO TRAIN / TEST (Week 3)
   - 90% training, 10% test
   - set.seed() for reproducibility
   - NEVER use test data during preparation
    ↓
4. BUILD THE MODEL (Weeks 3–6)
   - Choose algorithm (Decision Tree, Logistic Regression, Random Forest, GBM...)
   - Train on training data only
    ↓
5. EVALUATE (Weeks 3–4)
   - Predict on test data
   - Confusion matrix → TP, TN, FP, FN
   - Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, AUC
    ↓
6. IMPROVE
   - Feature engineering (most impactful)
   - Try different algorithms
   - Handle class imbalance (upsample/downsample)
   - Cross-validation for better evaluation
   - Hyperparameter tuning (last step!)
    ↓
7. EXPLAIN TO BUSINESS PEOPLE (Week 7)
   - Translate metrics into money, customers, risk
   - Never say "F1 score" to a manager
   - Say: "Of every 100 sick patients, our model finds 80 of them"
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Professor's Key Lessons — Things to Never Forget

These come directly from 15 years of industry experience. Read them carefully.

**On data:** "Feature engineering is more important than your choice of algorithm." A well-prepared dataset with a simple model beats a poorly-prepared dataset with a sophisticated model every time.

**On evaluation:** "Accuracy is only valid if the cost of all errors is equal." For fraud, disease, or any rare-event problem, accuracy is meaningless. Use Precision, Recall, F1, or AUC.

**On overfitting:** "If your training accuracy is much higher than your test accuracy, your model has memorised the data, not learned from it." This is the most common mistake in machine learning.

**On hyperparameters:** "Many people jump to hyperparameter tuning as their first step. It should be their last step. Fix your data first."

**On communication:** "Too many data scientists cannot talk to business people. If you cannot explain what your model does in plain English, it will never get deployed." Every technical result must be translated into business language: money saved, customers retained, risks avoided.

**On data leakage:** "Always split first, prepare second. If you use test data to inform your preparation steps, you are cheating — and your model will fail in the real world."

**On outliers:** "Do not delete outliers just because they look unusual. Ask yourself: could this value appear in future real data? If yes, keep it. If no (e.g., it is a data entry error), remove it."

**On clustering:** "Never expect equal cluster sizes. The small clusters are usually the most interesting."

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## A Quick Reference: Which Algorithm for Which Problem?

| Situation | Best Starting Point |
|---------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Predict yes/no (balanced data) | Logistic Regression or Random Forest |
| Predict yes/no (imbalanced data) | GBM or Random Forest with upsampling |
| Predict a number | Linear Regression or Random Forest |
| Need to explain logic in simple if-then rules | Decision Tree |
| Need maximum accuracy on tabular data | GBM or Random Forest |
| Find natural groups in data | K-Means or Hierarchical Clustering |
| Detect unusual records | PCA + Reconstruction Error |
| Recommend items to users | Apriori (basket-based) or Collaborative Filtering (user-based) |
| Reduce number of columns | PCA |
| Visualise high-dimensional data | t-SNE |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Algorithms Compared — One Table

| Algorithm | Type | Interpretable? | Speed | Best For |
|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|
| Linear Regression | Supervised (Regression) | ✅ Yes | Fast | Predicting numbers |
| Logistic Regression | Supervised (Classification) | ✅ Yes | Fast | Binary classification baseline |
| Decision Tree | Supervised (Both) | ✅ Very | Fast | Explainable rules, data exploration |
| Naive Bayes | Supervised (Classification) | Partly | Very fast | Text, spam, small data |
| SVM | Supervised (Both) | ❌ No | Slow on big data | High accuracy, medium datasets |
| Random Forest | Supervised (Both) | Partly | Medium | Most problems — reliable and powerful |
| GBM | Supervised (Both) | ❌ No | Slow | Best accuracy on tabular data |
| Neural Network | Supervised (Both) | ❌ No | Slow/very slow | Images, text, huge datasets |
| K-Means | Unsupervised | Partly | Fast | Customer segmentation |
| Hierarchical | Unsupervised | Partly | Slow on big data | When you do not know K |
| PCA | Unsupervised | ❌ No | Fast | Reducing dimensions |
| Anomaly Detection | Unsupervised | ❌ No | Medium | Finding unusual records |
